content.text: Sunan an-Nasā'ī is a collection of hadīth compiled by Imām Aḥmad an-Nasā'ī (rahimahullāh). It is unanimously regarded as one of the six canonical collections of hadith (Kutub as-Sittah) and contains roughly 5,700 hadīth (with repetitions) arranged in 52 books.

Aḥmad ibn Shu'ayb ibn 'Alī ibn Sīnān Abū 'Abd ar-Raḥmān al-Nasā'ī (214–303 AH / c.829–915 CE) was born in Nasa, Khurasan. He specialized in the study of Hadith from an early age and began travelling at twenty, visiting Iraq, Kufa, Hijaz, Syria and Egypt before eventually settling in Egypt.

Imam an-Nasa'i was renowned for his exceptional memory, piety and devotion. He regularly fasted, worshipped throughout the night, performed Hajj frequently, participated in Jihad and followed the Sunnah closely in his daily life.
His teachers included Ishaq ibn Rahweh, Imam Abu Dawud as-Sijistani and Qutaibah ibn Saeed. After settling in Egypt he became widely known as Hafidh al-Hadith.

items: Imam Abu Bakr Ahmad ibn Muhammad (Ibn as-Sunni)
Imam Tahawi also narrated from him.

content.text: Many scholars considered him a follower of the Shafi'i school, while others regarded him as Hanbali. It is also likely that he was an independent mujtahid with a leaning toward the Hanbali school.
He authored many valuable works on Hadith and Islamic sciences.

items: Sunan al-Sughra (Al-Mujtaba / Al-Mujtana), popularly known as Sunan an-Nasa'i

content.text: After compiling Sunan al-Kubra, he prepared a shorter selection containing authentic narrations known as Sunan al-Sughra (Al-Mujtaba). This work became famous as Sunan an-Nasa'i and is highly regarded for its authentic narrations and clear identification of weak reports.

Following persecution, Imam an-Nasa'i travelled from Palestine intending to reach Makkah. He passed away on Monday, 13 Safar 303 AH at the age of 88. According to Ibn Hajr and adh-Dhahabi, he died during the journey and was buried between Safa and Marwa in Makkah.
